questions.text: A supermarket sells its used refrigerant to another company. Before that refrigerant can legally change hands for reuse, it must be:

options.t: Reclaimed — reprocessed to AHRI 700 purity and chemically verified, typically by an EPA-certified reclaimer
Recycled — run through an on-site recycling machine with fresh filter-driers
options.why: Recycling only cleans refrigerant enough for reuse within the same owner's equipment. Crossing an ownership boundary is exactly what triggers the reclaim requirement.

options.t: Recovered — as long as it was removed with certified recovery equipment, it can be resold as-is
options.why: Recovery is just removal and storage — no processing at all. Recovered refrigerant cannot enter the market without reclamation.

options.t: Re-blended by any wholesaler to match the original composition specification
options.why: "Re-blending by a wholesaler" is not a Section 608 category. Purity restoration to AHRI 700 with lab verification is what the rule requires — that is reclamation.

questions.why: The three R's differ by destination: recover = remove/store; recycle = on-site cleanup for same-owner reuse; reclaim = AHRI 700 purity + chemical analysis, required before refrigerant changes ownership.

questions.text: You are recovering R-22 from a 300-lb commercial system using a recovery machine built in 2020. Per EPA, you must evacuate the appliance to:

options.t: 10 inches Hg vacuum
0 inches Hg (atmospheric) — that is the R-22 requirement
options.why: 0 in Hg applies to HCFC-22 appliances containing LESS than 200 lbs. At 300 lbs you are in the ≥200-lb row: 10 in Hg.

options.t: 500 microns
options.why: Microns belong to dehydration/evacuation before recharging — an industry practice, not an EPA recovery level. The recovery table is written in inches Hg.

options.t: 15 inches Hg vacuum
options.why: 15 in Hg is the deepest table value (very-high-pressure/other rows) — over-recall. The R-22 ≥200 lbs row with a post-1993 machine is 10 in Hg.

questions.why: EPA required-evacuation table: HCFC-22 appliance ≥200 lbs, recovery equipment manufactured after Nov 15 1993 → 10 in Hg vacuum. (<200 lbs → 0 in Hg.) Separate concept from the 500-micron dehydration pull.

questions.text: Which of these releases is actually LEGAL under the Section 608 venting prohibition?

options.t: The de minimis refrigerant that escapes while making a good-faith connection or disconnection during recovery
Venting R-410A, because it contains no chlorine and does not harm the ozone layer
options.why: The venting prohibition was extended to HFC substitutes back in 1995. Zero ODP does not make release legal.

options.t: Venting a small A2L charge, because its GWP is under the 700 threshold
options.why: The GWP-700 line is an equipment-manufacturing rule (Technology Transitions), not a venting exemption. Venting A2Ls is illegal.

options.t: Blowing refrigerant through a clogged filter-drier to clear debris from the line
options.why: Using system refrigerant as a "blow-out gas" is a knowing release — a classic exam distractor and a real fine in the field. Use recovered charge + nitrogen instead.

questions.why: Legal releases are a short, specific list: de minimis escape during good-faith recovery practices, nitrogen holding charges, and a few EPA-exempted substitutes in listed applications. Everything else stays in the system or the tank.

questions.text: When filling a recovery cylinder, you stop at 80% of its capacity. Why 80%?

options.t: To leave expansion room so rising temperature cannot hydrostatically over-pressurize the cylinder
To keep the cylinder light enough for one-person DOT-legal lifting
options.why: Ergonomics is not the rule's basis — liquid expansion is. A hydrostatically full cylinder can rupture with lethal force as temperature rises.

options.t: Because the last 20% of any cylinder is non-condensables by design
options.why: Non-condensables are a contamination issue diagnosed by pressure checks — nothing to do with the fill limit.

options.t: It is only a manufacturer recommendation; filling to 95% is acceptable when the tank is kept cool
options.why: The 80% fill limit is a hard safety rule (DOT/EPA exam material), not a soft recommendation — "keeping it cool" is not a control you can guarantee in a truck.

questions.why: Liquid refrigerant expands with temperature. The 80% limit preserves vapor space so the cylinder cannot go hydrostatically solid and rupture. Pair it with: DOT-approved cylinder, never mix refrigerants, transport upright.
questions.ref: EPA 608 Core cylinder safety; DOT 49 CFR

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of Section 608 of the Clean Air Act as it applies to HVAC technicians?

options.t: To minimize the release of regulated refrigerants during service, maintenance, repair and disposal of appliances
To set the minimum efficiency (SEER2/HSPF2) ratings that new equipment must meet
options.why: Efficiency standards come from DOE, not Section 608. Section 608 is about preventing refrigerant emissions.

options.t: To license contractors to run an HVAC business in a given state
options.why: Business licensing is a state matter. Section 608 is a federal technician certification about refrigerant handling.

options.t: To certify that a technician can size and design duct systems correctly
options.why: Duct design is a mechanical-code/ACCA topic. Section 608 governs refrigerant emissions and handling.

questions.why: Section 608 of the Clean Air Act exists to reduce emissions of regulated refrigerants (originally ODS, now including HFC substitutes) during service, maintenance, repair and disposal — the reason recovery, leak-repair and venting rules exist.
questions.ref: Clean Air Act §608; 40 CFR 82 Subpart F

questions.text: A supplier asks to see your certification before selling you a jug of R-134a. Why?

options.t: The Section 608 sales restriction generally limits refrigerant sales to certified technicians
They are just being cautious — there is no federal rule about who can buy refrigerant
options.why: There is a specific sales restriction: refrigerant (including HFC substitutes since 2018) may generally be sold only to certified technicians.

options.t: Only R-22 and other ozone-depleting refrigerants are sales-restricted; HFCs like R-134a are not
options.why: The restriction was extended to non-exempt HFC substitutes in 2018 — R-134a is covered.

options.t: The restriction only applies to cylinders larger than 50 lbs
options.why: The restriction is about the certification of the buyer, not a cylinder-size threshold.

questions.why: The Section 608 sales restriction limits sales of regulated refrigerants (extended to non-exempt HFC substitutes in 2018) to Section 608- (or 609-) certified technicians — which is why suppliers ask for your card.

options.t: Self-contained recovery equipment has its own compressor and can remove refrigerant from an appliance with a dead compressor
System-dependent recovery can be used on any appliance, including one with a failed compressor
options.why: System-dependent (passive) recovery relies on the appliance's own working compressor — it cannot pull from a dead system, and is limited to small appliances / working systems.

options.t: Recovery equipment made after Nov 15 1993 has no EPA performance requirements
options.why: Post-1993 recovery equipment must meet EPA performance standards (and be EPA-certified for sale) — that vintage line is exactly what the exam tests.

options.t: All recovery equipment is system-dependent by design
options.why: Self-contained machines with their own compressor are the standard for dead systems; system-dependent is the limited passive case.

questions.why: Self-contained recovery equipment has its own compressor/vacuum capability and works on dead systems; system-dependent recovery uses the appliance's own compressor (small appliances / working systems only). Post-Nov-15-1993 equipment must meet EPA performance standards.

questions.text: Who is responsible for keeping records of refrigerant added to and recovered from a large commercial appliance?

options.t: The owner/operator of the appliance must keep servicing and refrigerant-tracking records
No one — recordkeeping ended when the ozone rules were replaced by the AIM Act
options.why: Recordkeeping obligations continue under the current rules for larger appliances — they were not eliminated.

options.t: Only the refrigerant manufacturer keeps any records
options.why: The owner/operator keeps service and leak-rate records; the manufacturer is not the record-holder for a specific installed appliance.

options.t: Records are optional unless the local fire marshal requests them
options.why: Records are a standing federal requirement for larger appliances, not something triggered only by an inspection request.

questions.why: Owners/operators of larger appliances must keep records of servicing, refrigerant added/recovered and leak-rate calculations; technicians keep proof of certification; reclaimers and disposers have their own record duties. Retention periods are set by rule.
questions.ref: EPA 608 recordkeeping requirements (40 CFR 82 Subpart F)
